title: Dart Control Flow Mastery Test
description: Test your understanding of conditional statements, loops, branching, and flow control mechanisms in Dart programming

questions.explanation: The first condition (x > 5) is true (10 > 5), so it executes the first block and prints 'A'. The else-if is skipped even though it would also be true.
hints: Only the first true condition executes

questions.text: Which loop is best suited when you know the exact number of iterations in advance?
questions.explanation: The for loop is ideal when you know the exact number of iterations because you can initialize a counter, set a condition, and update the counter in one line.

questions.text: What does the 'break' statement do inside a loop?
questions.explanation: break immediately terminates the entire loop and continues execution with the statement following the loop.
The loop runs while i < 5. i starts at 0 and increments each iteration: 0,1,2,3,4 (5 iterations). When i becomes 5, condition fails.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'continue' statement?
options: Skips the remaining code in current iteration and moves to next
Restarts the loop from first iteration
questions.explanation: continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration of the loop.
When i=1, continue skips print(i). So only i=0 and i=2 are printed: 0 and 2.
hints: continue skips the print for i=1

questions.text: Which statement allows you to check a variable against multiple possible values?
questions.explanation: switch-case is specifically designed to check a single variable against multiple constant values, providing cleaner syntax than long if-else chains.
hints: Better than multiple if-else for many conditions

questions.text: What happens if you omit 'break' in a switch case?
options: Execution falls through to next case
questions.explanation: Without break, execution 'falls through' to the next case. Dart requires explicit break unless you want fall-through behavior.
do-while executes the code block once before checking the condition. Even though i < 5 is false (10 < 5 = false), the loop still executes once.
Outer loop runs i=0,1. For each i, inner loop runs j=0,1. So outputs: i=0,j=0='00'; i=0,j=1='01'; i=1,j=0='10'; i=1,j=1='11'
hints: Inner loop completes fully for each outer iteration

questions.text: What does the 'assert' statement do in control flow?
options: Checks a condition and throws if false (development only)
questions.explanation: assert evaluates a condition and throws an AssertionError if false. It only runs in development mode and is ignored in production.
x=5 matches case 5, which falls through to case 4's block (no break between 4 and 5), so it prints 'Medium'. Multiple cases can share the same block.
hints: Cases without break execute next case
